brief_document.title: Approval gate example with signals and timer timeout
brief_document.purpose: Human-in-the-loop workflows are a core use case for durable execution — the workflow pauses for hours or days waiting for a human decision, and the engine must keep it alive without holding resources. Developers need a concrete example showing how to use signals to receive external input (approve/reject), durable timers for deadline enforcement, and branching logic on the outcome. This pattern appears in every approval chain, review gate, and escalation flow.
brief_document.task: Create an approval-gate example that defines a Gleam workflow using the aion_flow SDK. The workflow submits a document for approval, waits for an approval signal with a configurable timer timeout, and branches on the outcome: approved routes to a publish activity, rejected or timed-out routes to an archive activity. Include a gleam.toml with the aion_flow dependency and a README explaining the signal/timer pattern and how to run the example.

requirements.title: Signal-based approval gate with timer timeout
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a workflow using workflow.define() that calls signal.receive() to wait for an approval signal. THE SYSTEM SHALL start a durable timer using timer.start() with a configurable timeout duration. THE SYSTEM SHALL use concurrency.race() to resolve whichever completes first — the signal or the timer.
acceptance: approval_gate.gleam imports from aion_flow and uses workflow.define().
The workflow calls signal.receive() to wait for an 'approval_decision' signal.
A durable timer is started with timer.start() using a configurable timeout.
concurrency.race() is used to race the signal against the timer.
The workflow survives engine restart and resumes waiting without re-executing completed steps.

requirements.title: Branching on approval, rejection, or timeout
requirements.spec: WHEN the approval signal carries an 'approved' payload, THE SYSTEM SHALL execute a publish activity. WHEN the signal carries a 'rejected' payload or the timer fires first, THE SYSTEM SHALL execute an archive activity. THE SYSTEM SHALL record the outcome as the workflow result.
acceptance: An 'approved' signal payload causes the publish activity to execute.
A 'rejected' signal payload causes the archive activity to execute.
Timer expiry (no signal received) causes the archive activity to execute with a timeout reason.
The workflow result includes the decision outcome (approved, rejected, or timed_out) and the action taken.

requirements.title: Project setup and documentation
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a gleam.toml with the aion_flow dependency and a README explaining the signal/timer approval pattern, how to send signals to a running workflow, and step-by-step instructions for building and running the example.
acceptance: gleam.toml lists aion_flow as a dependency and has correct project metadata.
README.md explains the human-in-the-loop pattern and why durable timers matter for deadlines.
README.md shows how to send an approval signal to the running workflow (via CLI or client SDK).
README.md provides build and run instructions including how to observe the timeout path.

boundaries: SHALL NOT modify the aion_flow SDK, engine, or server code — only consume them.
SHALL NOT add dependencies beyond aion_flow to the Gleam example.
SHALL NOT implement a real document management system — stub activities are sufficient.
SHALL NOT require an external service to send signals — the README shows how to send them via the Aion client or CLI.
verification: gleam build succeeds in the approval-gate directory without warnings.
The .aion package can be built from the compiled source.
The workflow starts and blocks waiting for a signal.
Sending an 'approved' signal causes the publish activity to execute and the workflow to complete.
Sending a 'rejected' signal causes the archive activity to execute and the workflow to complete.
Letting the timer expire causes the archive activity to execute with a timeout reason.
README instructions are complete and accurate.
